path: 99 Stuff/ETFA25 AAS Paper/ETFA25-000262 Faster Implementation Time Through Interoperability - Let’s Prove it.pdf
text: Faster Implementation Time Through Interoperability: Let’s Prove it! 1st Johannes M¨aule Fraunhofer Institute for Factory Operation and Automation IFF Magdeburg, Germany johannes.maeule@iff.fraunhofer.de 2 nd Frank Leinenbach Institute for Nondestructive Testing Fraunhofer IZFP Saarbr¨ucken, Germany frank.leinenbach@izfp.fraunhofer.de 3 rd Thomas Leich Automation and Informatics Harz University of Applied Sciences Wernigerode, Germany tleich@hs-harz.de 4th Christian Diedrich Chair of Integrated Automation Otto Von Guericke University Magdeburg, Germany christian.diedrich@ovgu.de Abstract—Standardized connections are intended to reduce implementation time. This is achieved through approaches such as the Asset Administration Shell (AAS). Although there is a substantial amount of literature on how this can be accom- plished with solutions like the AAS, there are no quantitative studies indicating the extent to which standardization can reduce implementation time. We aim to demonstrate this through an experiment. We believe it is important to engage the community in a critical discussion about the experiment design before we proceed with its execution. This paper examines two designs of experiments to measure implementation time in comparison to manufacture-specific interfaces. Many issues concerning the setup are open for discussion. It becomes clear that to achieve a valid measurement, multiple experiments must be conducted at various field levels and with different experimental subjects. We want to encourage researchers to engage this dialogue and these studies. Future research will execute the presented experiments. Index Terms—Asset Administration Shell, Digital Twin, Inter- operability, Implementation, Integration I. INTRODUCTION Plug & Produce approaches are designed to significantly shorten implementation times. The concept of the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) seeks to achieve this through standardization [1]. Similar machines of the same type have consistent data structures and APIs, thereby simplifying the work for system manufacturers. As shown in Figure 1 this enables system integrator to uniformly control machines of the same type with previously different manufacturer APIs. Utilization of AAS is well described in literature. How- ever, there is currently no quantitative evidence that this approach has advantages over manufacture-specific integration approaches. Evidence is needed to support the utilization of the AAS across the industry. Therefore this works aims to validate this approach by contribute • A first experiment design to measure the time to integrate an AAS comparing to manufacture specific APIs. This work was supported by the Fraunhofer flagship project EMOTION. • A second experiment design to measure the time integrate multiple AAS comparing to manufacture specific APIs. These two experiments should be conducted where a value is queried (READ) and an operation is to be performed (WRITE). Numerous issues emerged during the design phase, which will be thoroughly discussed here. In upcoming studies, we will execute these experiments and share the findings. We encourage researchers to evaluate and debate the designs of experiments critically. Fig. 1. Every manufacturer uses their own APIs. The AAS as a software layer uses standardized APIs to enable access to data. II. RELATED WORKS Empathic systems consist of several subsystems with mutual understanding and joint actions of all involved actors, making empathy a crucial capability of resilient production systems [2]. Instead of conventional status data, an EMOTION vector is exchanged, representing current requirements, states, historical data, environmental data, and events of interest [3]. The AAS can simplify data exchange for each actor, but its effectiveness is uncertain. In the field of machine communication, there are only a few quantitative studies. For example, performance benchmarking exist for OPC UA [4] and common fieldbuses [5]. In the area of AAS Braunisch (2023) compared various AAS SDKs on their consistency with the official specification of the AAS according to IDTA [6]. Associated with this is the test frame- work with which AAS SDKs can be tested [7]. Furthermore, Wei (2024) compares three AAS-related concepts [8]. Neither in the area of AAS nor in the field of machine communication protocols is the implementation time compared, nor does it go beyond a qualitative comparison. III. HYPOTHESES The AAS is characterized as a method to decrease time investment [9]. Due to the lack of quantitative studies, the presented designs aims to measure whether machines can be implemented faster through an AAS. We formulate two hypotheses for this. Hypothesis 1 (H1): A machine or system is implemented faster when using the AAS API compared to a manufacturer- specific API. This first experiment can determine whether the machine can be integrated more quickly using the AAS. However, it is expected that the AAS approach will only become profitable once it is rolled out. It could be even possible that the AAS approach performs even worse at first. As shown in Figure 2, the implementation time decreases as the quantity of implementations increases, thanks to accumulated experience through standardization. Therefore, we propose a second hypothesis: Hypothesis 2 (H2): The more often the same AAS is imple- mented, the lower the implementation effort compared to a manufacturer-specific API. Fig. 2. Implementation time will be reduced during roll-out. IV. INTEGRATION STEPS TOWARDS INTEROPERABILITY This section is intended to describe the steps necessary for a successful system integration. IEC 62390 defines six different compatibility levels to integrate a system in such a way that it can be replaced by an equivalent system [10]. The experiment setups presented in Section IV are based on these steps. Five levels are illustrated in Figure 3. To achieve inter- changeability devices must possess specific characteristics Coexistence is achieved when a common communication protocol is established, defined by the first to fourth layers of the OSI reference model (Transport Layer). Systems are connectable if they use the same communica- tion interfaces and the same data access methods. Communi- cation interfaces are defined by the fifth to seventh layers of the OSI reference model. To ensure consistent data access, the namespace must be uniformly defined. Additionally, the access direction (Read/Write) and the rhythm of data exchange must also be defined. In addition to cyclic data exchange, event- based communication is common. Fig. 3. Compatibility levels towards Interchangeability according to IEC 62390 [10] Two systems are considered interworkable when both data access and data types are clearly defined. The modeling language, such as JSON or XML, must also be specified. Interoperability between two systems is achieved when they can collaborate effectively. Functions for executing operations must be defined both syntactically and semantically. These functions are typically tailored to specific use cases, such as in operation or maintenance, and described with parameteri- zation. The highest level of compatibility is interchangeability, where one system can be replaced by another. This is possible when, in addition to the previous characteristics, the dynamic behavior can also be mimicked. V. EXPERIMENT DESIGNS To measure the implementation time of AAS compared to other implementation methods, we introduce two experiment designs in this section. The first experiment design aims to determine whether the machine integration using AAS is faster or slower compared to the usual integration methods from their suppliers. The second experiment aims to determine how the implementation time changes when two assets of the same class but from different manufacturers are used. A. Core Design Principles In Section IV, the essential aspects towards interoperability were described. The experiments designs should be based on these aspects. Communication Protocol The communication protocol or API is currently manufacturer-specific. Using AAS, the IDTA spec- ifies the API. Currently, the IDTA only specifies the HTTP protocol [11]. For an HTTP protocol, it is necessary to define the HTTP method, REST path, and parameters. The REST path is determined by the structure of the submodel. The submodel is standardized and published by the IDTA [12]. The IDTA describes so-called operations that can trigger functions. Operations can be parameterized through input and output variables. The HTTP method is defined in the Specification of the AAS Part 2. The user needs the knowledge to understand the AAS submodels and the AAS specifications. To make a meaningful comparison, the HTTP pro- tocol will also be used in these experiments. This approach ensures that any differences observed are attributed to the standardized APIs of the AAS, rather than the communication protocol itself. For manufacturer-specific APIs, users need documenta- tion that defines the HTTP methods, REST paths, and parameters. Data Access To ensure data access, manufacturers usually im- plement a role-based system. In addition to the documentation for this data access, the integrator needs the access credentials. Looking on AAS the security aspects including access control has been be specified in Part 4 of the IDTA Specification for AAS [13]. In the experiment designs, comparing security aspects is not feasible; consequently, they will be excluded. Data Types The data types depend on the data value. This applies to both the manufacturer-specific API and the AAS. The AAS adopts the data types as defined in IEC 61360 [14, pp. 31-33]. To ensure that the impact of different data standardization does not distort the results, the setup should ensure that both the manufacturer-specific data source and IEC 61360 are compliant. Semantics Once the API, including data access and data types, has been described, the crucial question arises re- garding the clarification of semantics. In most cases, manufacturers describe the meaning of the values in the documentation. The AAS provides the op- tion of so-called semantic IDs for its submodel elements, which refer to an internal or external ontology. For example, the semantic ID “0173- 1#05-AAA480#005” is an IRDI and belongs to the ECLASS ontology. There it is defined as ”0,001- fold of the SI base unit metre” [15]. A property can have multiple semantic IDs. However, the AAS sub- model specification includes not only the semantic ID but also a description text for system integrators. Application Functionality To ensure application functionality functions for ex- ecuting operations must be defined both syntacti- cally and semantically. Manufacturers describe their functions in documentation provided to the customer. The IDTA specifies that there should be mandatory submodels for the same asset class that must be utilized. Currently, no submodels exist for reading operational data and for issuing commands. These would need to be developed in the initial phase based on IDTA standards. In the experiment designs for implementing an asset with a manufacturer-specific API, the test subject requires docu- mentation. This documentation should include all information about each function, including its REST path, HTTP method, and HTTP parameters. Additionally, for READ operations, the test subject needs details about the REST path, HTTP method, and HTTP parameters necessary to retrieve data from the machine. To implement an asset using an AAS, the test subject must have knowledge of the developed asset-specific IDTA submod- els. Additionally, the subject requires information about the specifications of the AAS and its APIs. The metrics to be evaluated include the time required to successfully complete READ and WRITE operations. Addi- tionally, we will analyze how frequently the test subject are looking into documentation, submodel templates, and AAS specifications. Fig. 4. Cross-Over experiment design to Compare integration AAS versus manufacture-specific integration method. The two groups should take turns operating a machine using both the manufacturer-specific APIs and the standardized APIs of the AAS. Regarding the asset object, we do not want to make a definitive decision at this point. One consideration is using two Automated Mobilized Robots (AMRs) from two different manufacturers, provided they share the same capabilities so that the proposed experiments can be conducted. However, we are also considering other asset objects. B. Experiment to measure the time to integrate an AAS comparing to manufacture specific APIs The first experiment presented aims to verify Hypothesis 1, which evaluates whether the integration of an asset is faster when using manufacturer-specific APIs or standardized AAS APIs. We utilized a standard 2 × 2 crossover design for this experiment. As illustrated in Figure 4, this setup comprises two phases and requires two groups of participants, referred to as Group A and Group B. Group A will begin with machine integration via the manufacturer-defined API, while Group B will start with machine implementation using the AAS. Afterward, the groups will exchange roles: Group A will integrate the same machine again using the AAS, while Group B will perform the integration using the manufacturer- defined API. The crossover design offers a key advantage: it not only enables precise results with a limited number of participants but also reduces inter-subject variability in comparisons between groups and accounts for the influence of covariates [16]. For instance, participants may have prior knowledge of one method or the other. The crossover design helps to minimize this type of error. The integration aims to achieve two things: first, a specific operation should be performed (WRITE). Secondly, predefined values should be read (READ). The time taken by participants to complete both tasks will be measured. C. Experiment to measure the time integrate multiple AAS comparing to manufacture specific APIs As discussed in Section II, it is expected that there will be a dramatic time benefit as the number of implementations in- creases. Therefore, a further experiment is needed to compare two different machines of the same type and function to verify Hypothesis 2. The experiment design is illustrated in Figure 5. Group A will integrate two different machines of different types using manufacturer-specific APIs, while Group B will integrate the same machines using the AAS APIs. As in the first experiment, the same WRITE and READ operations will be performed while measuring the implemen- tation time. VI. DISCUSSION Although there have been numerous publications on the AAS and interoperability in recent years, no experiments of this kind have been conducted or proposed so far. Therefore, we aim to address and discuss several critical points in this section. For better overview we have spitted these issues into three categories: Fig. 5. Experiment design to measure implementation time during multiple integration processes. Two groups will integrate a machine: one group will use the respective manufacturer-specific APIs, while the other group will utilize the standardized APIs of the AAS. 1) Interoperability-specific issues that arise when a stan- dardized interoperability solution like AAS is used 2) AAS-specific issues that arise from the use of AAS. 3) Experiment-specific issues that arise from the experiment designs. The following section discusses the issues, with Figure 6 providing an overview of them. A. Interoperability-specific Issues 1) Integration costs of the AAS are not taken into account. This brings us to the next issue: we are measuring only the integration time without considering the overall integration effort. Thus, the creation of the APIs or the AAS itself is overlooked. It must be acknowledged that adhering to a specific standard for creating APIs is more time-consuming than simply inventing the APIs on the fly. We initially find it challenging to measure the effort involved in creating APIs, especially since this relates to knowledge, competence, experience, and appropriate tools in the context of the AAS. It will also be challenging to describe the benefits, as the integration effort for the system integrator decreases with each implementation due to the accumulation of experience Fig. 6. Identified and clustered Issues by discussing presented experiment designs (see Figure 2). At the same time, it would certainly be desirable to capture this effort. However, we emphasize that there are currently no quantitative studies available, making it valuable to quantify the benefits of the AAS, even if the associated effort remains unaccounted for at this stage. 2) Level of implementation does effect implementation time. The level at which an AAS is integrated makes a significant difference. Integrating the AAS with an MES for condition monitoring at a control technology level requires lower demands compared to direct integration at the PLC level. This would also require an implementation within the device. Both (as well as other) implementation levels should be evaluated distinctly and thoughtfully. 3) Limitations while focusing just one asset class. The next issue we wish to address is the selection of the assets to be tested. As previously mentioned, we will focus on two different assets from different manufacturers that share the same functionality. The IDTA refers to asset classes. For our experiments, we will initially restrict ourselves to a single asset class. These experiments can and should be extended to other asset classes to validate the results. The more assets that are analyzed, the stronger the conclusions will be. B. AAS-specific Issues 4) The advantages of AAS are not just implementation time. The most important point concerns the nature and benefits of the AAS itself. A shorter implementation time is only one aspect. The Reference Architectural Model Industry 4.0 (RAMI 4.0) has been used as a framework to outline the dimensions of the AAS, although it is worth noting that RAMI 4.0 is not without its flaws [17]. By focusing solely on machine connectivity, we are only addressing a small part of what the AAS is actually designed for. The advantage of the AAS, which takes the entire lifecycle of the asset into account, remains overlooked. Furthermore, the AAS serves as a means of interoperable communication beyond company boundaries, which we are also neglecting. Therefore, one could argue that our experiments represents only a partial aspect of the AAS. However, reducing integration effort is still significant! We believe that quantitative results are also needed for these other dimensions to demonstrate that the AAS is advantageous in these aspects as well. Indeed, there are no quantitative studies available in any of the other dimensions either. Therefore, we are focusing on integration time in this context. 5) Submodels are not yet standardized. Another weakness is that there are currently no asset-specific submodels that permit this. There is the submodel ”Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles in Intralogistics“ [12], but it only covers technical static data. Another submodel is ”Control Configuration and Parameterization for NC/CNC Machines“ [12]. This submodel enables the exchange and configuration of NC programs. For our approach, we would need asset control submodels for all asset types that are up to date with the latest technological standards. Existing IDTA submodels, such as the ”Asset Interface Description“ submodel or ”Control Component Instance“ [12], will be considered. However, the issue of standardization applies to all interoperability - standardization solutions. With these experiments, we aim to demonstrate the value of standardization and hope to generate support for standardization efforts. 6) HTTP unsuitable for event-based communication. In the presented experiments, we only use the HTTP protocol. We argue that this enables a better comparison, ensuring that we do not measure impacts related to a specific communication protocol. The HTTP protocol presents several disadvantages. At lower implementation levels, as discussed in Issue 3), it is rarely utilized. Additionally, HTTP is not suitable for reading event data. Indeed, communication protocols based on data publish-subscription are rightly gaining traction for read operations. However, there are currently no standardized APIs for this event-based communication in relation to the AAS. Additionally, it would need to be determined which event- based protocol should be used. Therefore, it seems reasonable to limit this experiment to HTTP in the initial stage. 7) No Utilization of Industry 4.0 Language. The utilization of the Industry 4.0 Language enables plug & produce. By standardizing an interaction protocol, AAS can be queried based on existing capabilities. This principle eliminates any integration work. However, while this approach is well described in the literature, there is also a lack of quantitative studies in this area. For our approach, we are initially focusing on reactive AAS. 8) No consideration other IIoT standardization solutions such as OPC UA. At this point, we are considering only the AAS as a stan- dardization solution. We recognize that other standardization solutions, such as OPC UA, are available, but we are not aware of any quantitative studies in this domain either. The methodology proposed here can certainly be used to compare OPC UA with manufacturer-specific APIs, and we encourage researchers to explore this. We encourage researchers to pursue this. Whether these experiments is suitable for measuring the integration time of AAS compared to OPC UA would require a separate paper. As a first step, it should be tested whether and to what extent standardization solutions are suitable for reducing implementation time. C. Experiment-Specific Issues 9) Selection of test subjects Selecting test subjects and determining the sample size is certainly a challenge. While the crossover design reduces the confounding factors resulting from the varying competencies of individuals, it does not completely eliminate these confounding factors. It seems logical to recruit experienced system integrators as test subjects. On the other hand, it could also be interesting to select less skilled individuals. In this case, students could be used for simplicity. We believe a test group should consist of at least ten individuals. 10) Execution problems During the experiment, various issues may arise. For instance, the test subjects might not fully understand the assigned tasks, the documentation, or the specifications of the AAS. Additionally, technical problems can occur. To mitigate these challenges, we aim to ensure thorough preparation and clear documentation. It should also be noted that test subjects tend to behave differently when under observation compared to real-life situations. This impacts the reliability of the results. 11) Carry-Over Effect It is important to recognize that the crossover design takes into account that the execution of the first phase affects the participants [16]. Implementing the first phase naturally develops competencies that provide advantages for the second phase. This could be problematic if one approach results in greater learning effects than the other. D. Experiment Conditions Based on the discussion, we derive the following conditions for the experiment to be conducted: • The communication protocols of the machines and sys- tems to be integrated must be comparable. Ideally, the same one should be used. • The manufacturer-specific APIs require documentation that indicates how to perform the required READ and WRITE operations. • A submodel based on the IDTA submodels is required for the asset under consideration. • The test subjects must possess sufficient knowledge for the experiment to be successful about: – the asset to be integrated, – the communication protocols to be used, – the specifications and the structure of the AAS. They must understand how a submodel is implemented and structured. VII. CONCLUSION This work presents two experiment designs to measure the implementation time of two different implementation methods. The fact that the discussion section is as lengthy as the presentation of the setup highlights the numerous issues that need to be considered. It is unlikely that these two exper- iments will definitively prove that standardization reduces implementation time; several experiments will be necessary. Different implementation levels, communication protocols and asset types must be explored. In future works, we will conduct the presented experiments in coordination with the community. In studies on empathetic production systems, we want to demonstrate the benefits of integrating empathy through AAS. We encourage the research community to collaborate and establish these experiments on a broader scale. Ultimately, it is clear that the efforts in both research and industry to achieve interoperability through standardization are of limited value unless we can demonstrate their benefits. The proposed method has the potential to provide that evidence. REFERENCES [1] L. M. V. Da Silva, A. K¨ocher, M. S. Gill, M. Weiss, and A.
